Digital Data Capture & Analysis Lab Book

Edward Mason

Weeks 1 and 2: Creating a 360 Virtual Tour of Campus

This week we have been using ThingLink and 360 photos taken with GoPro/Insta360 cameras to create 360 Virtual Tours.

Our tour is of the Y3 building on the University campus. We started in the basement, going “up” the stairs and out of building, moving past the front of the building and back in via another door. We then went back into the basement via the lift, and then finishing in one of the seminar rooms. We used a GoPro Max camera on a tripod, and connected it via Bluetooth to an iPhone with the GoPro Quik app on for remote control of the shutter.

I then uploaded the footage to ThingLink to create the tour. The platform was fairly intuitive to use, but it was a slow and repetitive task to go through each image in turn, changing settings and adding “tags” to enable moving between each image. Furthermore, there were some shots with people in, which were blurred out for privacy reasons. Viewing it with a VR headset was impressive, despite the low resolution of the images.

A virtual tour such as this would be useful for familiarising people with (for example) a campus, office space, or a new development. However, ethical concerns such as photographing of bystanders, as well as taking photos of sensitive sites should be considered.

Below is the final tour on ThingLink.

Figure 1: A virtual tour of University of Birmingham Y3 Building

Weeks 3 and 4: Photogrammetry (Structure for Motion modelling)

Figure 2: A model of an IKEA Marius stool

The model above is of an IKEA Marius stool in my living room. Overall, I would say this model is quite poor, as it does not adequately represent the stool.

Firstly, the mesh of the model is quite poor, leading to holes and floating geometry, as ghost points are formed. There was also erroneous mesh points formed from carpet and wall shots. As well as this, the texture of the model is very poorly captured, with none of the black colour of the stool in the model.

Both of these issues could be due to the lack of a plain background (making it more difficult for the software to distinguish what the focus is), a poor photo distribution (i.e. the photos did not provide sufficient coverage of the stool), or possibly poor camera quality. The other point to consider is the software used - an open-source package called Regard3D. Alternative methods of processing could be considered, such as modifying parameters, however considering the aforementioned background concerns, these would probably be not as consequential in approving the final model output.

Weeks 5 and 7: Enivronmental Sensing

Turning to environmental sensors, we looked into how open-source hardware and software and how their use has lowered the barriers to sensing. This opens up possibilities to non-profits, for example, to use sensors to back up their concerns with data. In our case, we used a temperature sensor, composed of a thermistor attached to an Arduino micro-computer (see Figure 1). The resistance across the thermistor changes with temperature, and therefore if this resistance is measured a temperature can be calculated from it.

Figure 3: Temperature Sensor Schematic


Calibration

The first part of the process involved calibration. Using four water baths at different temperatures, the resistance read by the Arduino-based sensor was recorded along with the temperature from a reference probe, with ten readings taken from each bath. The results are shown in Figure 2:

Figure 4: A graph to show Resistance against Temperature as measured by an Arduino-based temperature sensor


We can see a clear pattern of an increase of resistance as temperature decreases - this means that the thermistor used has a negative temperature coefficient (an NTC thermistor).

Next, we will fit a calibration model to the data. The code below creates a linear and non-linear (polynomial) model from the data.

linear_model <- lm(data = df_cal, 
                   formula = Ref_temp ~ Resistance)

poly_model <- lm(data = df_cal, 
                 formula = Ref_temp ~ poly(
                   x = Resistance, degree = 2, raw = T))

The summaries are shown in the table below:

Table 1: Calibration Model Summaries
Model Residual Std. Error Multiple R-Squared F-Statistic and p-value
Linear Model 3.784 (28 df) 0.9434 466.4, p < 2.2e-16
Polynomial Model 0.2554 (27 df) 0.9998 5.424 × 104 , p < 2.2e-16

The summary statistics from this model shows that the polynomial model is a better fit to the data, with a smaller residual standard error, and a larger R-Squared value and F-Statistic.

Below are the models plotted against the data:

Figure 5: A graph to show Resistance against Temperature with a linear model

Figure 6: A graph to show Resistance against Temperature with a non-linear model

The mean and standard deviation for each model is shown below.

Table 2: Summary Statistics for each Reference Temperature and Model
Ref Temp (°C) Beta Model (Mean ± SD °C) Linear Model (Mean ± SD °C) Poly Model (Mean ± SD °C) Log Linear Model (Mean ± SD °C)
2.6 5.271±0.056 0.415±0.135 2.6±0.02 2.113±0.073
16.2 17.167±0.031 21.34±0.038 16.202±0.044 17.012±0.037
39.5 37.036 36.779 39.691 39.348
39.6 37.192±0.088 36.849±0.04 39.825±0.075 39.512±0.092
39.7 37.173 36.841 39.809 39.492
39.9 37.219 36.862 39.848 39.54
40.0 37.136 36.824 39.777 39.452
40.7 36.853 36.695 39.532 39.154 
df_cal_long <- df_cal |> pivot_longer(!c(Cal_medium, Resistance,
                                       Voltage, Ref_temp, 
                                       Log_res),
                                    names_to = "cal_model", 
                                    values_to = "cal_temp")
beta_model <- ThermistorTemperature(R0 = 10000, T0 = 25, R = df_cal$Resistance, betaTH = 3950)

ggplot(data = df_cal_long,
       mapping = aes(x=Ref_temp, y = cal_temp,
       colour = cal_model))+geom_point(size=2)+
  facet_wrap(~cal_model,scales = "fixed")+
  theme_pubclean()+
  geom_abline(slope = 1)+
  geom_smooth(se = F,method = "lm",linetype = "dashed")+  
  stat_regline_equation(label.x=5, label.y=40)

Figure 7: Models Comparison 

summary(beta_model)
summary(linear_model)
summary(poly_model)
summary(log_linear_model)

rmse(df_cal$Ref_temp, df_cal$Temp_beta)
rmse(df_cal$Ref_temp, df_cal$Temp_linear)
rmse(df_cal$Ref_temp, df_cal$Temp_poly)
rmse(df_cal$Ref_temp, df_cal$Temp_loglin)
Table 3: Statistical Variables for Each Model
Variable Beta Model Linear Model Poly Model Log Linear Model
R2 N/A 0.9434 0.9998 0.9983
RMSE 2.253915 3.655814 0.2423138 0.6319984
Slope 0.86 0.94 1 1

Table 3 shows that the sensor is quite accurate in calculating temperature, especially when paired with a polynomial model. Whilst this may not be as accurate as a commercial sensor, it gets incredibly close and for most projects this should be fine.

Todo

  1. Give this section of your lab book a title – e.g “using environmental sensors”.

  2. Briefly explain how you collected the data (i.e. the methods you used). Here you can include images, schematics and text. Be sure to highlight the instruments used.

  3. For the calibration generate a scatter plot of resistance vs observed temperature. Ensure you include a figure caption and some plain text with a brief description of the pattern(s) observed.

  4. Now fit a linear model and a non-linear model. Extract information on the model fit and present these in a table with a short interpretation of the results (which model appear to have the better fit)?

  5. Apply both models to the data and calculate the mean and SD for each calibration data point. Present this as a table, what do you notice about the data?

  6. Calculate the sensor linearity and accuracy (RMSE) based on the model you think is best. Present this data as a table or integrated into a short paragraph that describes the results. Would you be happy to use this sensor for another research project? How does it compare to commercial sensor specifications?

  7. For the ADC experiment plot the data as boxplot (8-bit, 10-bit and 12-bit). What do you notice? Now plot the data as a dot plot – what does this tell us about the difference in the step size (i.e. resolution in °C)?

  8. Calculate the mean and SD for each resolution along with the mean and SD of the reference sensor (10 data points required). What do you notice?

  9. End your notebook with some short reflections on using the Arduino platform – what worked well, what didn’t and what you’d do differently if you were using the platform for another project.

The Arduino platform is a well-developed “open-hardware” ecosystem with supporting software and documentation. However, as with all “open-source” systems, there are some pitfalls. Firstly, the system is very much DIY - you are provided with the components, but as the user you must then assemble the parts, both physically (the hardware) and in terms of programming the assembled system to do what you would like it to. This requires, in the case of the Arduino ecosystem, a knowledge of C/C++ languages. Furthermore, if anything goes wrong, there is no dedicated customer support, but only documentation and support forums. That being said, if these obstacles can be overcome, the Arduino platform can scale rapidly and relatively cheaply compared to alternative proprietary sensing systems, making it a viable option for more widespread sensing networks, or for smaller companies and/or non-profits who cannot afford the more expensive alternatives.

Week 6: APIs and AI - Using Flickr’s API to map sentiments and topics spatially in Sutton Park, West Midlands

Looking at how to use APIs and AI, this next section shows how the use of Flickr’s API can show in a visual way how people experience green spaces, with very precise spatial data.

Flickr is an interesting data source for understanding people’s experience of green spaces. It provides a wide variety of tagged photos, across a wide temporal and spatial range. These tags give an indicator of what people associate with that particular photo. Furthermore, location tags and timestamps give a precise point in space and time when this occurred. However, it worth noting that the platform of Flickr is mostly the realm of photographers, and is not as synonymous as a platform such as Instagram.

Figure 10: A map showing the locations of photos of Sutton Park (Source: Flickr)

There is an interesting spatial pattern for the cluster “People and the Park”, which clearly shows an outline of major paths in the park. Furthermore, the “Wildlife” cluster is very centred around the SE of the park. It is also worth noting that a shortfall of the machine learning algorithm used to form these clusters may result in more positive or negative clusters, potentially due to how it links similar words together.

Figure 11: A graph to show sentiment analysis of the photo data of Sutton Park

To further this project, a data source such as Instagram may be useful due its more widespread use, however their API is much more difficult to access. Also of use may be a review platform, such as Google Maps.

Final Project: Using GTFS Feeds to Visualise Transport Data from the West Midlands

Project Aim: Using Transport for West Midlands’ GTFS feeds and the tidytransit R package (Poletti et al. 2026), this project shows how service frequency and transport mode varies across the West Midlands Combined Authority area.

Todo

  1. Briefly explain what you’re trying to achieve with the project

  2. Explain the choices you made on the techniques used

  3. Reflect on any ethical implications

  4. Critically evaluate the effectiveness of your project

References

Poletti, Flavio, Daniel Herszenhut, Mark Padgham, Tom Buckley, and Danton Noriega-Goodwin. 2026. Tidytransit: Read, Validate, Analyze, and Map GTFS Feeds. https://github.com/r-transit/tidytransit.
